title: Move.toml pins pas/ptb to branch 'main' — non-reproducible build
description: Both PAS and PTB git dependencies are pinned to `rev = "main"` (a branch ref) rather than a commit SHA. Any commit to `main` upstream silently changes dependency behavior. Move.lock has it pinned to b64f0c58c6... for the testnet environment, so existing builds resolve deterministically, but a fresh clone on a different environment (mainnet) or after a lockfile regeneration will pick up whatever `main` points to at that moment.
impact: Reproducibility and supply-chain risk. Upstream PAS can change semantics (e.g. approval-stamping, clawback validation) without any local code change. Auditors cannot guarantee the audited version matches a future deployment.
recommendation: Replace `rev = "main"` with the pinned SHA from Move.lock (`b64f0c58c60888de99ad9c5a72e9f0289aa7b6fc`). Add a CI check that fails if any `git` dep in Move.toml uses a branch name rather than a 40-char SHA. Consider tag-based pinning once PAS publishes versioned releases.
evidence:
pas = { git = "git@github.com:MystenLabs/pas.git", subdir = "packages/pas", rev = "main" }
ptb = { git = "git@github.com:MystenLabs/pas.git", subdir = "packages/ptb", rev = "main" }

title: hadron::Move.toml edition is '2024.beta' — should be stable '2024'
description: The hadron package declares `edition = "2024.beta"`. The stable Move 2024 edition has shipped; other in-repo packages (token_template, token_template_pas) already use `edition = "2024"`. The pas upstream itself still uses `2024.beta`, which may have forced the choice — but hadron should verify it builds under the stable edition and migrate.
impact: Minor. Staying on `2024.beta` leaves the package on a pre-release edition with no guaranteed forward compatibility. Inconsistent edition between hadron (`2024.beta`) and its sibling token_template_pas (`2024`) complicates reasoning.
recommendation: Migrate Move.toml to `edition = "2024"` and re-run `sui move build` to confirm nothing breaks. If the upstream PAS package `2024.beta` declaration blocks upgrade, track the upstream migration.
evidence: edition = "2024.beta"

title: register_pas_asset lacks explicit is_pas_coin<T>() guard — opaque abort on double registration
description: `register_pas_asset<T>` does not explicitly check whether T is already registered (either as regulated via admin::register_coin or as PAS via a prior register_pas_asset call). It relies on `policy::new_for_currency` aborting via `EPolicyAlreadyExists` (upstream policy.move L51) and/or `treasury.add_pas_coin_caps` aborting via `ECoinAlreadyRegistered` (treasury.move L169). Each abort surfaces from a different module, making failures hard to diagnose.
impact: Operational confusion. Different failure modes depending on whether the prior registration was as a regulated coin (Hadron-side abort), a prior PAS registration (PAS-side abort at `EPolicyAlreadyExists`), or both. Callers trying to re-register can't easily infer root cause.
recommendation: At the top of `register_pas_asset`, add explicit early checks: `assert!(!treasury.is_coin_registered<T>(), ECoinAlreadyRegistered); assert!(!treasury.is_pas_coin<T>(), ECoinAlreadyRegistered);` — return Hadron's error consistently.

title: whitelist mutators key-lookup DOF for non-PAS coins aborts opaquely
description: `add_to_whitelist`, `remove_from_whitelist`, `set_whitelist_send`, `set_whitelist_receive` accept `Auth<WhitelistManagePermission, T>`. `auth::new_auth` asserts `treasury.is_coin_registered<T>()` (i.e. any coin with a TreasuryCap DOF) — but NOT `is_pas_coin<T>()`. If a T was registered via `admin::register_coin` (regulated, non-PAS), an Auth<WhitelistManagePermission, T> can be minted, and the call then aborts deep inside `dynamic_object_field::borrow_mut` because `WhitelistKey<T>` DOF does not exist. The abort surfaces as a framework error, not a Hadron error.
impact: Operational misdiagnosis: compliance officers get cryptic errors on mis-typed coin. More critically, the permission system's invariants become hazy — a whitelist-manage role nominally granted for "PAS operations" implicitly covers regulated coins too, just with non-functional outcomes.
recommendation: In each of the four whitelist mutator paths (and in `treasury::add_to_whitelist`/`remove_from_whitelist`/`set_whitelist_send`/`set_whitelist_receive`), add an explicit `assert!(treasury.is_pas_coin<T>(), ENotPasCoin)` check at the top. The error constant already exists in treasury.move L67. Alternatively, extend `auth::new_auth` with an `is_pas_coin` check when the permission is `WhitelistManagePermission` (less clean, conflates concerns).

title: approve_transfer accepts any Treasury holding PAS T — no binding to coin issuer
description: `approve_transfer<T>` is permissionless (intentional — autoresolve callable by the PAS SDK). It receives `treasury: &Treasury` and consults its whitelist for sender/recipient. There is no assertion that THIS treasury actually issued PAS T. If no treasury issued T as PAS, the underlying `dynamic_object_field::borrow` inside `assert_can_send/assert_can_receive` will abort. But more subtly: if a `Request<SendFunds<Balance<T>>>` exists for a T issued by some other institution's treasury but an attacker points `approve_transfer` at a different treasury that also happens to hold a whitelist DOF keyed by T, the behavior is undefined at the treasury-ownership level. In practice, only one Treasury can hold `TreasuryCap<T>` at any time, so only one treasury can carry the whitelist DOF keyed by `WhitelistKey<T>` — upstream OTW uniqueness rescues this. But the code does not document or assert this invariant.
impact: Silent reliance on OTW uniqueness. If any future code path allows a treasury to hold a stray `WhitelistKey<T>` DOF without owning TreasuryCap<T>, the whitelist of that treasury could gate a policy that a different treasury owns. Current code prevents it, but the surface is fragile.
recommendation: Add `assert!(treasury.is_pas_coin<T>(), ENotPasCoin)` at the top of `approve_transfer`. This makes the precondition explicit and fails-closed with a clean error. Also add a comment referencing upstream `policy::new_for_currency` L51 which enforces one-policy-per-type-via-namespace.

title: approve_transfer gated by is_mint_redeem_paused — conflates mint/redeem with transfer pause
description: Transfers are blocked when mint/redeem is paused for the PAS asset. The spec language and the error message `EMintRedeemPaused: "Mint and redeem operations are paused for this PAS asset"` describe a mint/redeem pause, but the check also disables user-to-user transfers. This is a design choice but: (1) the error text misleads — a user hitting this won't know why their transfer failed if the intent was to pause only issuance; (2) there is no independent "transfer pause" lever. If an operator wants to halt issuance while letting existing holders transfer out, they cannot.
impact: Policy ambiguity. Pausing mint/redeem to throttle supply also freezes the entire transfer graph. Conversely, unpausing transfers requires also unpausing mint/redeem. Either this conflation is intentional (document it) or a separate `is_transfer_paused` lever is needed.
recommendation: Option A (minimal): rename the error to `EMintRedeemOrTransferPaused` and update the string to reflect that pausing mint/redeem also halts PAS transfers; document the conflation in the module header. Option B (cleaner): introduce a `TransferPausedKey<T>` and `PauseTransferPermission` separate from mint/redeem pause so compliance can halt issuance without freezing circulation.
evidence:
assert!(!treasury.is_mint_redeem_paused<T>(), EMintRedeemPaused);
    treasury.assert_can_send<T>(sender);

title: approve_transfer validates wallet addresses but funds land at recipient_account_id
description: Hadron's `approve_transfer` validates `request.data().sender()` and `request.data().recipient()` — these are wallet/object addresses. Upstream `send_funds::resolve_balance` deposits funds at `recipient_account_id.to_address()` (see /Users/alilloig/workspace/pas/packages/pas/sources/requests/send_funds.move L69-70). The recipient_account_id is set when the Request is created by `account.send_balance` as `namespace::account_address_from_id(from.namespace_id, to)` — deterministically derived from the wallet `to`. So the wallet check is effectively equivalent. HOWEVER, `account.unsafe_send_balance` (upstream account.move L111-123) lets the caller pass an arbitrary `recipient_address`, which becomes both the wallet field AND the derivation seed for the recipient_account_id. The wallet check still holds in that case. So the design is sound under the current upstream contract, but it is implicit and could silently break if upstream changes the relationship between `recipient` and `recipient_account_id`.
impact: Tight coupling to upstream PAS internal invariants. If upstream introduces a send variant where `recipient` and `recipient_account_id` can be decoupled (e.g. a sponsor-address variant, or a forwarding account), Hadron's whitelist would enforce on the wrong address without local code changes.
recommendation: Add a defensive assertion that the recipient_account_id equals the derivation of recipient: assert `request.data().recipient_account_id() == namespace_account_id(namespace_id, recipient)` or at least document the invariant. Also validate `sender_account_id` similarly. Reference /Users/alilloig/workspace/pas/packages/pas/sources/account.move L170-185 and send_funds.move L62-71.

title: pas_supply::burn receives Request<ClawbackFunds> — permissionless initiator can drain accounts if burn succeeds
description: Upstream `account::clawback_balance<C>` (account.move L99-106) takes NO auth — anyone can initiate a clawback Request against any account. The Request cannot be dropped (no `drop` ability), so the PTB must resolve it. Hadron's `burn` accepts any such Request as long as the caller also has `Auth<RedeemPermission, T>`. This means: a Hadron-issued RedeemPermission holder can construct a PTB that calls `account::clawback_balance` on ANY account (including unfriendly users) and then `pas_supply::burn` to destroy those funds. There is no check that the source `from` address in the Request was authorized or is even whitelisted. The design intent (from the orchestrator LEADS) is that burn is an admin operation and the redeem-holder is trusted. Still, this reads as "RedeemPermission = burn-any-user's-balance", which may surprise operators who expect redeem-style symmetric semantics (user-initiated).
impact: A compromised or malicious redeem role can erase any holder's PAS balance without their consent. This is likely the intended compliance behavior (an issuer-initiated destruction) but the naming `redeem` and the lack of event detail (only `from`/`amount` is emitted; no initiator address) muddy the audit trail.
recommendation: (1) Document in the module header that RedeemPermission for PAS is issuer-initiated destruction (clawback-burn), not user-redeem — and thus requires stricter role-assignment than a regulated-coin redeem. Consider renaming `burn` to `clawback_burn` to match upstream terminology. (2) Emit the initiator address in PasBurned (currently only emits `from` and `amount`). (3) Consider whether the caller should need both RedeemPermission AND the source `from` to be whitelisted (or at least emit an event with the source's whitelist status at time of burn).

title: pas_supply::mint does not assert is_pas_coin — relies on missing whitelist DOF for non-PAS fail-closed
description: `mint<T>` requires `Auth<MintPermission, T>`, which is mintable for any registered coin — PAS or regulated. If somebody pushes a regulated coin T via pas_supply::mint, the flow: (1) `assert_mint_redeem_enabled<T>` OK, (2) `assert_can_receive<T>(account.owner())` aborts in `dynamic_object_field::borrow` because `WhitelistKey<T>` DOF does not exist for a non-PAS coin. Fail-closed, but with an opaque framework error. The complementary `supply::mint` does have `assert!(!treasury.is_pas_coin<T>(), EPasCoinNotAllowed)` (supply.move L35). The reverse check is missing.
impact: Inconsistent error handling symmetry: PAS coin mint via `supply::mint` → clean `EPasCoinNotAllowed`; regulated coin mint via `pas_supply::mint` → cryptic framework abort.
recommendation: Add `assert!(treasury.is_pas_coin<T>(), ENotPasCoin)` at the top of `pas_supply::mint`, `pas_supply::burn`, and `pas_supply::seize` for parity with supply.move's guard.

title: WhitelistKey docstring says `Table<address, bool>` — actual type is `Table<address, WhitelistEntry>`
description: The `WhitelistKey` docstring describes the DOF value as `Table<address, bool>`. The actual type (per treasury.move L233-237 and L260) is `Table<address, WhitelistEntry>` where `WhitelistEntry { can_send, can_receive }` is a struct with two booleans. Stale doc left over from an earlier iteration.
impact: Reader confusion; maintenance risk when a future dev edits keys.move and assumes the documented type.
recommendation: Update the docstring: `/// DOF key for Table<address, WhitelistEntry> whitelist (PAS assets).`
evidence:
/// DOF key for Table<address, bool> whitelist (PAS assets).
public struct WhitelistKey<phantom T>() has copy, drop, store;

title: can_send/can_receive/is_whitelisted abort on non-PAS T — not pure predicates
description: `is_whitelisted<T>`, `can_send<T>`, `can_receive<T>` borrow the whitelist table via `dynamic_object_field::borrow`. If T is not a PAS coin for this treasury, the DOF does not exist and borrow aborts — despite these being marketed as predicates that `return bool`. Any caller using them expecting a fail-safe false will instead abort.
impact: Off-chain view call over non-PAS T aborts; on-chain check-before-mutate idioms can't be written safely (e.g. `if (treasury.is_whitelisted<T>(addr)) { ... }` cannot be used defensively).
recommendation: Make predicates fail-safe: early-return false when `!is_pas_coin<T>()`. Example: `public fun is_whitelisted<T>(treasury: &Treasury, addr: address): bool { if (!is_pas_coin<T>(treasury)) return false; let wl = dynamic_object_field::borrow(...); wl.contains(addr) }`. Keep `assert_can_send/receive` strict (they already assert).

title: add_to_whitelist defaults both can_send=true and can_receive=true — surprising default for receive-only use cases
description: `add_to_whitelist` always initializes `WhitelistEntry { can_send: true, can_receive: true }`. There is no variant to add an address as receive-only (e.g. for an entity that should receive distributions but not transfer out). A compliance officer who wants to add a receive-only address must call `add_to_whitelist` then `set_whitelist_send(addr, false)` — two transactions (unless batched in a PTB), with a brief window where the address has send privileges it should not have.
impact: Temporary over-privilege if the two operations are not bundled in a single PTB; inconsistent with granular permission design elsewhere in the module.
recommendation: Provide an `add_to_whitelist_with_flags<T>(treasury, auth, addr, can_send, can_receive)` entry in pas_admin.move that initializes the entry with explicit flags in a single call. Keep the default-both `add_to_whitelist` for the common case.

title: WhitelistAdded event lacks can_send/can_receive flags — audit trail loss
description: `WhitelistAdded<T> { addr }` emits only the address. The entry is created with both flags true, but that invariant is implicit. If `add_to_whitelist_with_flags` is added (see R10-012), the event must carry the flags anyway — and even today, emitting `{ addr, can_send, can_receive }` makes the event self-contained for indexers and forensics.
impact: Indexers cannot reconstruct the can_send/can_receive state at time of addition without replaying subsequent `WhitelistSendUpdated`/`WhitelistReceiveUpdated` events; complicates compliance reporting.
recommendation: Add `can_send: bool, can_receive: bool` fields to `WhitelistAdded<T>` and `emit_whitelist_added<T>` signature; have `pas_admin::add_to_whitelist` pass them through.

title: PasMinted/PasBurned/PasSeized events omit actor (who triggered the action)
description: `PasMinted<T>` carries `recipient, amount` but not the minter. `PasBurned<T>` carries `from, amount` but not the redeemer. `PasSeized<T>` carries `from, to, amount` but not the seizer. Non-PAS equivalents `Minted<T>` include `minter: address` (events.move L79-82). This inconsistency makes PAS activity harder to audit.
impact: Weakened audit trail for regulated operations exactly where accountability matters most.
recommendation: Add `minter: address` to `PasMinted`, `redeemer: address` to `PasBurned`, `seizer: address` to `PasSeized`. Update emit helpers and call-sites in pas_supply.move to pass `ctx.sender()`. This may require threading `ctx: &TxContext` through to the event helpers (already available in burn/mint; seize takes no ctx — add one).

title: PasAssetRegistered event emits no metadata — no indication of clawback flag, treasury, or caller
description: `PasAssetRegistered<T>` is emitted as an empty struct (phantom T only). Registration is a major state transition that sets clawback policy, establishes the whitelist, and assigns caps — but the event records none of this. Compared to `TreasuryCreated { treasury_id, label, admin }`, PAS registration is under-instrumented.
impact: Indexers see "a PAS asset was registered" but must query additional state to learn which treasury, who called, and the policy settings. Makes event-driven compliance pipelines brittle.
recommendation: Include `treasury_id: ID, registrar: address, clawback_allowed: bool, policy_id: ID` in `PasAssetRegistered<T>`. Update pas_admin::register_pas_asset to pass them through.
evidence: public struct PasAssetRegistered<phantom T> has copy, drop {}

title: WhitelistManagePermission collapses four spec permissions — loses least-privilege granularity
description: Per the design spec distilled in the orchestrator context: `add_to_whitelist` should require `Auth<WhitelistAddPermission, T>`; `remove_from_whitelist` should require `Auth<WhitelistRemovePermission, T>`; `set_whitelist_send/receive` should require `Auth<WhitelistAddPermission, T>` (implicitly a modify-permission). The implementation uses a single `WhitelistManagePermission` for all four. This is a documented spec-drift — the consolidator should surface it as info/low.
impact: Operators cannot assign add-only, remove-only, or modify-only whitelist roles. A compliance officer who should only add addresses can also revoke addresses, which may conflict with internal separation-of-duty policies.
recommendation: If the spec drift is intentional (simpler operations model), document in the module header with a comment that `WhitelistManagePermission` is deliberately coarse-grained. If finer granularity is desired, split into three permissions: `WhitelistAddPermission`, `WhitelistRemovePermission`, `WhitelistModifyPermission` (for set_whitelist_send/receive). Align with orchestrator spec excerpt (section 7.2).
evidence:
// ============ PAS Whitelist ============

public struct WhitelistManagePermission {}

title: register_transfer_template uses TransferApproval<T> package address — post-upgrade templates go stale
description: `register_transfer_template<T>` computes `approval_type.address_string()` from `TransferApproval<T>` and hard-codes it into the template's `package_id`. When Hadron is upgraded to a new package address, `TransferApproval<T>` resolves to the new address but the already-registered template still points to the old package. `update_transfer_template<T>` exists to fix this, but it's not automatic: after any package upgrade, an operator MUST call `update_transfer_template` for every PAS asset or the SDK autoresolve will call the old package. If the old package still works, users silently bypass any new logic; if it's been made no-op or reverted, transfers fail.
impact: Post-upgrade operational hazard. Without tooling to batch-update templates for every registered PAS asset, some assets will have stale templates. If audits focus on the new package, stale-template transfers evade inspection.
recommendation: (1) Document in the module header that post-upgrade `update_transfer_template` MUST be called per asset; provide a script that enumerates PAS assets in a treasury and calls update for each. (2) Consider emitting a `TemplateUpdated` event so indexers can flag stale templates. (3) Longer-term: explore a derived-object-based template pointer that is recomputed from current package at resolve time (requires upstream PAS cooperation).

title: update_transfer_template emits no event — silent state change
description: `update_transfer_template<T>` is a significant operational event (redirecting autoresolve for a PAS asset after an upgrade) but emits nothing. Other state-changing paths in this module (register, whitelist mutations) all emit events.
impact: Indexers and compliance pipelines cannot detect post-upgrade template redirection. An adversary with RegisterCoinPermission could silently update the template to point to a malicious package — while other state changes are all logged.
recommendation: Add a `TemplateUpdated<T> { treasury_id: ID, new_package: address }` event and emit it at the end of `update_transfer_template`. Bonus: also emit from `register_pas_asset` so the first registration is recorded the same way.

title: pas_supply::seize takes treasury: &Treasury — assert_mint_redeem_enabled on an immutable ref works but semantics confusing
description: `seize<T>` takes `treasury: &Treasury` (immutable). Good — it doesn't need to write to treasury state. But `burn<T>` takes `&mut Treasury` only because `treasury_cap_mut` is needed. `mint<T>` takes `&mut Treasury` same reason. This is consistent but note: `seize` can thus run concurrently with other `&` readers (e.g. read-only queries), while `burn`/`mint` serialize. For a high-throughput seize workflow this is fine; just worth confirming the treasury's DOF layout allows `&Treasury` to borrow the policy/whitelist (it does — both are DOFs accessed via `dynamic_object_field::borrow` which only requires `&UID`).
impact: None at rest; informational.
recommendation: No action required. Document the rationale in the module header if readers might be confused why seize is `&` and burn is `&mut`.

title: No Move tests for any new PAS module (pas_admin, pas_supply, pas_transfer)
description: Scanned for `#[test]` annotations in the new PAS modules — none found. Coverage gap for: register_pas_asset happy-path and double-registration; add/remove/set whitelist transitions; mint with non-whitelisted account owner; mint with paused mint/redeem; burn with valid/invalid Request; seize to non-whitelisted recipient; approve_transfer with non-whitelisted sender/recipient; approve_transfer when paused; post-upgrade update_transfer_template flow.
impact: Zero regression safety. Any refactor, upstream-PAS change, or follow-up phase-3 feature could break PAS flows without detection.
recommendation: Add a dedicated `tests/pas_admin_tests.move`, `tests/pas_supply_tests.move`, `tests/pas_transfer_tests.move`. Use the test-helper scaffolding already present (`treasury::create_for_testing`, `version::init_for_testing`, `auth::create_auth_for_testing`). Cover: (1) register_pas_asset happy path; (2) double-registration aborts with ECoinAlreadyRegistered; (3) non-PAS coin whitelist mutation aborts cleanly; (4) mint to whitelisted/can_receive account; (5) mint to non-whitelisted address aborts with ENotWhitelistedReceive; (6) paused mint aborts with EMintRedeemDisabled; (7) burn with valid Request + matching Policy; (8) seize happy path and to-non-whitelisted abort; (9) approve_transfer happy path; (10) approve_transfer with non-whitelisted sender/recipient aborts.
evidence:
module hadron::pas_admin;

use hadron::auth::{Auth, TreasuryAuth};

title: pas.ts deployAndRegisterPasAsset uses string-substring type matching — fragile
description: Object ID extraction relies on substring matching framework type strings (`t.includes('::coin::TreasuryCap<')`, `t.includes('::coin_registry::MetadataCap<')`, `t.includes('::coin_registry::Currency<')`). If Sui framework ever moves these types to a different module path or renames them (unlikely but possible), the script silently misidentifies objects and the resulting IDs get passed to the wrong arguments in the PTB.
impact: If framework moves types, `treasuryCapObjectId` etc. end up empty strings and the guard at L81-87 catches it with a thrown error. But if one of the substrings matches by accident (e.g. a future type named `TreasuryCap2`), the wrong object is silently bound.
recommendation: Extract from parsed type structure instead of substring matching: use `@mysten/sui/utils::parseStructTag(type)` and match on `address === '0x2' && module === 'coin' && name === 'TreasuryCap'`. More robust and self-documenting.

title: pas.ts uses JS number for u64 amounts — precision loss above 2^53
description: `mintPasTokens(..., amount: number)` and the corresponding call arguments in `pasSupply.mint` pass a JS `number`. For PAS assets with large decimals (e.g. 6 decimals + large face-values), u64 amounts above `2^53 - 1` (9,007,199,254,740,992) will lose precision in the JS number path. The Mint Permission auth flow, BCS serialization, and the Move entry will all receive a silently truncated value.
impact: High-value PAS mints silently produce incorrect balances. With 6 decimals, this is ~9T tokens which is reachable for corporate bonds or ETF NAV denominated in base units.
recommendation: Change the `amount` parameter type to `bigint` (or `string | bigint`) and use `tx.pure.u64(BigInt(amount))` or let the codegen coerce. Update call-sites (full-setup.ts uses `1_000_000` which is safe; but the TYPE should not allow unsafe values). Apply the same fix to supply.ts (phase-1, but PAS flow depends on it).

title: pas.ts PASClient instance created at module load — runs on import even if unused
description: `const pasClient = new PASClient({ suiClient: getClient(NETWORK) as any });` executes at module-import time. If `getClient(NETWORK)` throws (misconfigured network / env) or PASClient constructor has side effects, every consumer of `pas.ts` incurs that even if they only import a single function. The `as any` cast also hides SDK type-mismatch bugs.
impact: Brittle import chain; harder-to-diagnose startup failures; type-safety escape hatch.
recommendation: Lazy-initialize per call, or accept the client as a parameter: `function getPasClient() { return new PASClient({ suiClient: getClient(NETWORK) as unknown as any }) }` and invoke inside each function. Investigate the `as any` cast — the correct fix is to align PASClient's expected SuiClient version with the one @mysten/sui 2.4.0 exports.
evidence: const pasClient = new PASClient({ suiClient: getClient(NETWORK) as any });

title: pkg() returns '' fallback — all PAS TS operations silently compose invalid PTBs if deploy-output missing
description: `pkg()` returns `CONSTANTS.package.latest || CONSTANTS.package.v1`. Both default to `''` if `readDeployOutput()` returns nothing (see L52-55). All PAS script functions (`registerPasAsset`, `addToWhitelist`, `mintPasTokens`) construct moveCalls with `package: pkg()` — if `pkg()` is `''`, the PTB is assembled with an empty package address. This will fail at execution time with an unclear error, rather than a clean upfront check.
impact: Operator debugging time: a missing `deploy-output.json` surfaces as a deep PTB error instead of "run `bun run deploy` first".
recommendation: Make `pkg()` throw if the package ID is empty: `export function pkg() { const p = CONSTANTS.package.latest || CONSTANTS.package.v1; if (!p) throw new Error('Hadron package not deployed — run `bun run deploy`'); return p; }`. Apply same to `CONSTANTS.versionObjectId` / `namespaceObjectId` fallbacks.

title: deployPasTokenTemplate hard-codes bytecode hex without source-of-truth script or regen check
description: `BYTECODE_HEX` is a single 1KB hex string embedded in source. There is no build script or comment linking it to the `token_template_pas/sources/token_template.move` it was compiled from. Divergence between the Move source in the repo and the bytecode actually deployed is undetectable without manual disassembly.
impact: Audit gap: a reviewer cannot verify that the bytecode matches the in-repo source without recompiling and byte-comparing. Post-audit, a malicious contributor could update the Move source without updating the bytecode, so deployments continue using the old bytecode silently (or vice-versa).
recommendation: (1) Add a script `scripts/src/deployPasTokenTemplate/regenerate-bytecode.ts` that builds `token_template_pas/`, extracts the bytecode, hex-encodes it, and writes it to `getBytecode.ts`. (2) Add a CI check that re-runs the script and diffs against the committed `BYTECODE_HEX`. (3) In the meantime, add a header comment in getBytecode.ts: `// Regenerated from token_template_pas/ at commit <sha>. Do not hand-edit.`

title: full-setup.ts passes unused moduleName: 'hadron_bond' — misleading about module path
description: The `TokenTemplateValues.moduleName` field is declared but not consumed by `patchConstants.ts` — the module name is derived from `symbol.toLowerCase()` (stripped of non-word chars). With `symbol: 'HBOND'`, the deployed module name will be `hbond`, not `hadron_bond`. Anyone reading full-setup.ts reasonably assumes the Move path would be `<pkg>::hadron_bond::HBOND` but it's actually `<pkg>::hbond::HBOND`.
impact: Developer confusion when reproducing the flow. Not a security issue; a docs/UX issue.
recommendation: Either (a) remove `moduleName` from `TokenTemplateValues` type since it's unused, or (b) use `moduleName` as the source-of-truth for `update_identifiers` in `patchConstants.ts` and stop deriving from `symbol`. Option (b) is more flexible but requires care to keep OTW struct name aligned with module name.

title: token_template_pas/Move.toml has empty [dependencies] — implicit Sui framework deps rely on 1.45+
description: `token_template_pas/Move.toml` declares `edition = "2024"` with empty `[dependencies]`. This requires Sui 1.45+ for implicit framework deps. Older toolchains will fail to build. No CI or README callout of this requirement.
impact: A fresh environment running an older `sui move` will fail to build the PAS token template. No explicit error pointing to toolchain version.
recommendation: Either (a) add explicit `Sui = { git = ..., rev = ... }` and `MoveStdlib` deps for broader toolchain compatibility, matching the pattern of the hadron root package; or (b) add a README section / CI step that asserts `sui --version >= 1.45`.
evidence:
[package]
name = "token_template_pas"
edition = "2024"

[dependencies]

title: token_template_pas/sources/token_template.move line 24 marked public(package) but deployed as 0x0:: — access modifier is moot
description: `init_treasury` is `public(package)` but the template module is published as a standalone package (`module 0x0::token_template`). No other module in this standalone package calls `init_treasury`. The visibility modifier has no effect (other than making the fn non-public-exported). This mirrors the phase-1 template pattern but is worth noting for cleanliness.
impact: None (behavior is unchanged). Code-quality polish only.
recommendation: Mark as `fun` (private) since it's only called within the module, or remove if unused outside tests. Align with Move best-practice: use the least-permissive visibility.

title: ClawbackApproval witness is public struct — any caller with RedeemPermission can auto-approve any clawback Request
description: `ClawbackApproval<phantom T>() has drop;` is declared `public struct`. While the witness can only be constructed inside `pas_supply` (Move privacy on the constructor `ClawbackApproval<T>()`), making it `public struct` means any module in the package could add the same constructor syntax if they share the package. More importantly, the permit pattern used for `TransferApproval` (via `internal::permit<TransferApproval<T>>()` in pas_transfer) is NOT used for `ClawbackApproval`. This asymmetry is subtle.
impact: Within-package it's fine — only pas_supply constructs the witness. But if a future developer adds a module that stamps `ClawbackApproval<T>()` (perhaps as a quick fix), the witness is leaked without a centralized review point. The transfer path is protected by internal::Permit; clawback is not.
recommendation: Option A: leave as-is but document that only pas_supply may construct ClawbackApproval<T>. Option B: mirror pas_transfer's pattern — make ClawbackApproval construction require an `internal::Permit<ClawbackApproval<T>>` that only pas_supply can issue, so any other module trying to stamp clawback requests is blocked at compile time. Option B aligns with upstream examples (kyc_registry uses permit for TransferApproval).
evidence:
/// Witness for PAS clawback approval. Required by the PAS Policy for clawback_funds action.
public struct ClawbackApproval<phantom T>() has drop;

title: add_pas_coin_caps does not validate metadata_cap corresponds to treasury_cap
description: `add_pas_coin_caps` stores `treasury_cap`, `metadata_cap`, `policy_cap` as DOFs but does not assert that `metadata_cap` corresponds to the same currency as `treasury_cap`. A malicious caller (with RegisterCoinPermission) could in principle pass a TreasuryCap<A> and a MetadataCap<B> (B != A) if they have both. The generic `<T>` forces both to be parameterized by T, so the type system rescues this — you cannot have MetadataCap<T> that refers to a different T. OK — the type-level binding suffices. But note: upstream `policy_cap` is `PolicyCap<Balance<T>>` which binds to Balance<T>, not T directly, so the type-level binding is slightly indirect.
impact: No adversarial path through current typing. Worth a comment stating the type-level invariant for future reviewers.
recommendation: Add a module comment on `add_pas_coin_caps` stating: `/// All three caps are bound to the same T via type parameterization; no runtime check is needed.` Consider `treasury_cap.total_supply() == 0` is the only runtime precondition — keep that assertion.

title: remove_from_whitelist does not clear in-flight approval state — but WhitelistEntry has no state so OK; document
description: `remove_from_whitelist` fully removes the entry (no soft-revoke). Since `WhitelistEntry` carries only `can_send, can_receive` (no timestamps, rate limits, or in-flight transfer state), there's no leftover state to clean. A re-addition will create a fresh entry with both flags default-true. Intentional but worth documenting to prevent future additions of stateful fields breaking this assumption.
impact: None today. Future-proofing documentation.
recommendation: Add a comment above the function: `/// Fully removes the entry; any future stateful fields in WhitelistEntry must also be cleared here.`
